Transition out
Beat: experiments will also reveal predictable failure modes—name them next


If you want faithful mimicry for an obscure author:

supply descriptors the discourse never standardized
include a short excerpt
anchor with contrasts (“closer to X than Y”)
specify constraints explicitly

You are, in effect, creating the missing discourse the model never had.


Failure modes taxonomy: what goes wrong and why

Section goal
Beat: give readers a diagnostic toolkit
Failure mode 1: generic output (weak index)
Beats: label is under-discussed; no stable descriptor bundle
Beats: remedy—add descriptors, exemplars, contrast anchors
Failure mode 2: parody/caricature drift (over-compressed index) — EXTRACTED: “Prompting becomes reductive for complex authors”
Beats: label evokes stereotypes; model overweights salient features — EXTRACTED: “caricature of the author’s most-discussed traits”
Beats: remedy—add “avoid parody” constraints; specify what not to do; provide exemplar
Failure mode 3: flattening of multi-register authors/genres — EXTRACTED: “Authors who write across genres are often flattened”
Beats: discourse stabilizes one dominant mode; the model reproduces that — EXTRACTED: “discourse tends to compress them into one dominant voice”
Beats: remedy—specify phase/register (“early vs late,” “news brief vs feature”) — EXTRACTED: “specifying which voice, period, or genre”
Failure mode 4: cue conflict (mixed signals)
Beats: incompatible constraints cause blended mush
Beats: remedy—declare priority ordering; separate passes (structure first, then style)
Failure mode 5: refusal / “cannot comply” that looks like incapacity
Beats: assistant layer may prohibit imitation or inference
Beats: remedy—reframe request as descriptors and goals rather than identity mimicry — EXTRACTED: “explicit descriptors outperform names for such authors”
Failure mode 6: style without truth (credibility illusion)
Beats: stance/genre cues can increase perceived authority without improving accuracy
Beats: remedy—add explicit epistemic constraints; require sourcing; require uncertainty marking
Transition out
Beat: now connect the phenomenon directly to journalism stakes—credibility and institutional power



Authors who write across genres are often flattened — When an author operates in multiple stylistic registers, discourse tends to compress them into one dominant voice. LLMs reproduce that compression, not the author’s full stylistic range.
Genre-hopping reduces stylistic addressability — Authors who deliberately avoid a stable signature style may be harder for models to mimic precisely, because discourse has less incentive to stabilize them into a single callable label.
Register shifts are learned unevenly — If an author’s stylistic modes are discussed asymmetrically (e.g., “early vs late,” “fiction vs essays”), the model will reproduce the most discursively salient mode, not necessarily the most representative one.
This favors authors with teachable styles over adaptive ones — Writers known for a consistent, nameable style are easier for models to reproduce than writers whose strength lies in flexibility, experimentation, or contextual adaptation.
Prompting becomes reductive for complex authors — Asking a model to “write like X” often produces a caricature of the author’s most-discussed traits, not a faithful recreation of their full practice.
It reframes authorship as a distribution, not a point — For multi-register authors, effective prompting may require specifying which voice, period, or genre—mirroring how human readers distinguish among phases of an author’s work.
It explains why explicit descriptors outperform names for such authors — When an author’s discourse identity is unstable or plural, direct stylistic constraints (“plainspoken, reportorial, emotionally distant”) outperform the name alone.



Why this matters for journalism: genre, stance, credibility, and institutional voice

Section goal
Beat: make the concept feel central, not decorative
[~] Journalism is label-saturated (beats) — EXTRACTED provides general framing: “compressed cultural labels,” “discourse stabilizes style”
[~] Beat: newsroom practice compresses genres into reusable names — EXTRACTED provides conceptual basis
Beat: those names carry structural, stylistic, and epistemic expectations
Three control targets to keep separate (subsections)
Style (surface)
Beats: sentence rhythm, diction, affect display
Structure (genre template)
Beats: ordering, formatting, attribution conventions
Stance (epistemic posture)
Beats: hedging, skepticism, neutrality signals, calls to action
Credibility implications (beats)
Beat: authoritative-sounding formats can mask weak evidentiary grounding
Beat: “institutional voice” can be reproduced without institutional practices
[~] Pedagogical implication (beats) — EXTRACTED: “prompting is cultural navigation, not instruction”
[~] Beat: students should learn to treat label prompts as rhetorical devices with power effects — EXTRACTED provides framing
Beat: evaluation must distinguish surface conformity from evidentiary quality
Transition out
Beat: now widen to the cultural politics of which voices become “callable” at all NOTE: Extracted content provides foundational mechanism; journalism-specific application still needs development



Style is learned from discourse, not just text — LLMs don’t learn an author’s voice only from their novels; they also learn it from how people talk about that author—reviews, criticism, teaching materials, comparisons, and advice.
Author names function as compressed cultural labels — “Hemingway” works as a stylistic prompt because human discourse has already reduced a complex body of work into a small, reusable bundle of traits (short sentences, restraint, concreteness). The model inherits that compression.
Discourse stabilizes style into a callable handle — Without sustained secondary discourse, an author’s name may not reliably activate a specific stylistic pattern—even if the author wrote extensively. Volume alone is not enough.
This explains why some names work and others don’t — Canonical, widely discussed authors act as clean style selectors; obscure or under-discussed authors often produce vague or generic results unless additional descriptors or examples are supplied.
It reveals that prompting is cultural navigation, not instruction — When we prompt with names, we are steering the model toward socially stabilized regions of language use, not invoking an internal “style engine.”
It clarifies differences between AI platforms — Some systems allow weak or inferred stylistic activation; others require explicit grounding to avoid hallucination. These are policy choices layered on top of the same underlying mechanics.



Cultural politics of indexability: canon formation as model affordance

Section goal
Beat: show that “what is easy to summon” mirrors cultural attention — EXTRACTED: “AI voice is a mirror of human attention”
[~] Discursive attention produces callable handles (beats) — EXTRACTED provides framing
Beat: widely discussed voices become highly indexable — EXTRACTED: “heavily discussed, taught, and compared become easier”
Beat: less-discussed voices become fuzzy or require user-supplied scaffolding — EXTRACTED: “marginalized or less canonized voices become harder”
[~] Two consequences framed neutrally but clearly (subsections) — EXTRACTED provides content, needs structuring
Canon amplification — EXTRACTED: “AI style is conservative by default”
Beats: stabilized labels get reinforced by ease-of-use — EXTRACTED: “preferentially reproduce stabilized, legible styles”
Marginalization by under-indexing — EXTRACTED: “discourse did less compressive work”
Beats: lack of secondary discourse reduces label reliability — EXTRACTED
Constructive response: descriptor-first de-canonizing prompts (beats)
Beat: use descriptive constraints instead of names
Beat: build “voice profiles” from observable features
Beat: treat names as optional shorthand, not primary control
Transition out
Beat: from critique to design—what tools and assignments should do differently



It reframes concerns about bias and canon formation — Voices that are heavily discussed, taught, and compared become easier for models to reproduce; marginalized or less canonized voices become harder—not because they are less “learnable,” but because discourse did less compressive work.
It reveals that AI style is conservative by default — Models preferentially reproduce stabilized, legible styles, which may unintentionally discourage stylistic ambiguity, hybridity, or evolution.
It changes how we should interpret AI pastiche — What looks like stylistic understanding is often the reproduction of discourse consensus, not engagement with the author’s full creative range.
It shows that AI voice is a mirror of human attention — AI voice mirrors literary reception, not literary intent. Models reflect how authors are received and categorized, not how they understand their own practice. What models can “sound like” is ultimately shaped by what humans have collectively named, debated, and agreed to recognize as a style.



Design and workflow implications: interfaces that expose control honestly

Section goal
Beat: propose practical patterns for tools and teaching — EXTRACTED: design/evaluate AI writing tools + tools for writers/critics
UI pattern 1: “descriptor panels” over “name panels”
Beats: show sliders/choices for sentence length, hedging, attribution, stance markers
Beats: reduce name-as-authority shortcut dependence — EXTRACTED: “Descriptors beat names for complex styles”
UI pattern 2: “active constraints display”
Beats: show which constraints are currently steering generation
Beats: separate style vs structure vs stance toggles
UI pattern 3: “two-pass generation workflows”
Pass 1: structural scaffold
Beats: produce outline/lede/body ordering
Pass 2: voice pass
Beats: apply style/stance constraints within fixed structure
Classroom pattern: “audit the label”
Beats: require students to list what the label implies
Beats: require students to specify which implications they want and which they forbid
Transition out
Beat: close by restating the central claim and giving readers a compact checklist NOTE: “Implications for writers” and “Unknown/emerging writers” content extracted below provides extensive material for this section



It changes how we should design and evaluate AI writing tools — Effective style control depends on supplying or selecting discourse, not just naming authors—suggesting new interfaces, safeguards, and pedagogical uses.
It suggests new tools for writers and critics — AI systems could expose voice as a spectrum or set of registers rather than a single label, aligning more closely with how many authors actually work.



Key Takeaways for Writers

Your “AI voice” is the most-discussed version, not your full range
Consistency increases imitability
Genre-hopping gets flattened
Reception matters more than intent
Descriptors beat names for complex styles
You can shape how AI sees you through interviews, essays, and criticism


Your “AI voice” is not your full voice — When a model writes “like you,” it reproduces the most-discussed version of your style, not the full range.
Consistency increases imitability — Writers with a stable, nameable style are easier for models to mimic.
Genre-hopping gets flattened — Models collapse multi-genre writers into a single dominant mode.
Reception matters more than intent — Models reflect how critics describe your work, not how you understand it.
Caricature is the default failure mode — Name-based prompting exaggerates surface traits.
Descriptors beat names for complex styles — Concrete constraints work better than your name alone.
Periods and projects matter — Prompting for specific phases aligns better with how models internalize variation.
Avoiding a signature style reduces AI mimicry — Resisting a consistent voice is an unintended form of stylistic protection.
AI favors legible styles over experimental ones — Easily summarized forms are more likely to be reproduced.
AI pastiche is about discourse, not creativity — AI imitates the shorthand others use to talk about your work.
You can shape how AI sees you—indirectly — Essays, interviews, and criticism influence how your work gets reproduced.

When an author provides writing samples to an LLM to establish “their style,” they are not teaching the model who they are in any durable sense. They are temporarily constructing a local stylistic attractor inside the current context.
Understanding that distinction changes how writers should approach the task.

1. You are not establishing a permanent style—only a local one
Providing samples does not create a new “author” inside the model. It creates a short-lived conditioning context that biases generation for the duration of the interaction.
Once the context window resets, the style disappears unless it is reintroduced.
Implication: think of samples as scaffolding, not identity.


2. The model learns patterns, not intention
The model does not infer:

what you are trying to do stylistically,
what you consider essential vs incidental,
or what you would reject as “not my voice.”

It only absorbs surface regularities:

sentence length,
punctuation habits,
cadence,
lexical choices,
degree of explicitness or restraint.

Implication: the samples must exhibit the voice you want, not merely represent your work broadly.


3. Samples compress your voice—often aggressively
When you provide examples, the model implicitly compresses them into a small set of dominant features. Subtleties are often lost; outliers are ignored.
Implication: choose samples that are internally consistent and representative of the specific register you want reproduced.


4. Mixing registers weakens the signal
If your samples span multiple genres or tonal modes, the resulting “style” will blur.
Implication: if you write in multiple voices, treat each as a separate conditioning task. Do not ask the model to infer plurality unless that plurality is the goal.


5. Short, dense samples often outperform longer ones
A few tightly focused paragraphs can be more effective than many pages, because they emphasize recurring features rather than narrative variation.
Implication: prefer compact samples with clear stylistic markers over long excerpts.


6. The model will reproduce habits, not judgment
LLMs replicate what appears often, not what appears intentionally. Tics, defaults, and structural habits are more likely to be copied than high-level craft decisions.
Implication: edit samples to remove things you don’t want amplified.


7. Explicit descriptors help stabilize what samples alone cannot
Combining samples with brief stylistic constraints (“restrained emotional register,” “short declarative sentences,” “avoid metaphor”) often produces better results than samples alone.
Implication: treat descriptors as guardrails, not redundancies.


8. Expect caricature unless you intervene
Without corrective feedback, models tend to exaggerate whatever features stand out most strongly in the samples.
Implication: iterative correction (“less clipped,” “fewer metaphors,” “more connective tissue”) is part of the process, not a failure.


9. Voice is easier to maintain than evolve
Once a style is established in-context, the model will resist gradual shifts unless explicitly instructed.
Implication: if your goal is stylistic evolution, you must guide it deliberately; otherwise the model will cling to the compressed version.


10. Think like an editor, not an author
The most effective way to work with LLMs on voice is to act as an external editor defining constraints, not as a creator expecting recognition.
Implication: success comes from curation, not self-description.


The key takeaway for unknown writers

Supplying writing samples does not teach a model who you are; it teaches it how to sound like the most compressible version of what you’ve shown.

Writers who understand this can use LLMs effectively—by shaping the compression rather than resisting it.


Takeaway checklist and closing synthesis

Compact restatement of thesis (one paragraph worth of beats) — EXTRACTED: “Language models don’t imitate authors. They reuse compressed cultural descriptions of style.”
Beat: labels steer by indexing stabilized bundles — EXTRACTED: “clean, callable style clusters”
Beat: discourse stabilizes bundles — EXTRACTED: “broadly discussed, taught, and compared”
Beat: platform layers gate behavior — EXTRACTED: “guess, ask, or refuse—depending on platform policy”
[~] Practical checklist (bulleted) — EXTRACTED provides “why this matters” list, needs reformatting
Beat: if label works too well → add anti-parody constraints and specificity
Beat: if label works poorly → add descriptors, exemplars, contrasts
Beat: if system refuses → reframe as explicit stylistic profile rather than name-based inference
Beat: always separate style/structure/stance and evaluate them separately
Closing beat — EXTRACTED: “stylistic prompting isn’t magic. It’s navigation through a landscape…”
Beat: control over outputs is partly control over discourse—what gets named, taught, repeated — EXTRACTED: “control over LLMs is partly control over discourse”


Authors who are broadly discussed, taught, and compared become clean, callable style clusters. Prolific but obscure authors often don’t—because models learn how humans talk about style, not just style itself.
Language models don’t imitate authors. They reuse compressed cultural descriptions of style.
Names work when they point to something humans have already agreed on. When that agreement is missing, models must either guess, ask, or refuse—depending on platform policy.
Seen this way, stylistic prompting isn’t magic. It’s navigation through a landscape shaped as much by human conversation as by neural networks.
And that landscape—what gets named, taught, compared, and remembered—quietly determines what AI can do well.
This explains:

Why some prompts feel “magical.”
Why authority tokens work better than rules.
Why pedagogy leaks into capability.
Why models reproduce cultural canons so strongly.
Why “style” is easier than “truth.”

And it suggests that control over LLMs is partly control over discourse, not architecture.


Glossary (restructured for this article)

[~] Discursive label — EXTRACTED provides related concept (discourse and compression)
Cultural compression — EXTRACTED: “Socially stabilized shorthand for a complex, contested, but repeatable practice”
[~] Discursive index — EXTRACTED provides framing (high-density index concept)
[~] Conditioning — EXTRACTED provides related concepts (latent concept representations)
Latent distribution (as shorthand) — EXTRACTED: latent concept representations and activation
Style vs structure vs stance (explicit triad)
Caricature/parody drift — EXTRACTED: feature superposition explains “parody happens”
Flattening — EXTRACTED: “styles blur,” “obscure authors don’t resolve cleanly”
Assistant layer / policy layer
Descriptor-first prompting
[~] Contrast anchoring — implied in Section 6 extractions NOTE: Extracted glossary provides technical background; article-specific definitions still needed


latent concept representations and activation (from NLP) Research into latent concept representations asks whether concepts like style, sentiment, or authorial voice exist as separable latent dimensions.
Key ideas: - Concepts are not symbols but directions or subspaces in embedding space. - Tokens like “Hemingway” activate those subspaces.
Relevant work: - Concept activation vectors (CAVs) (Kim et al.) - Amnesic probing (Elazer et al.) - Linear probes for style, genre, persona
But: this work usually treats concepts, not discursive labels, and misses the cultural angle.
feature superposition (from interpretability) This is where the mechanistic story is strongest.
Researchers like Chris Olah and collaborators study how:

Multiple unrelated features share neurons.
High-level abstractions emerge from overlap, not modular design.

Style clusters are classic examples of superposed features:

syntax
cadence
lexical choices
affect

An author name doesn’t toggle a module; it biases a mixture.
This explains why:

styles blur
parody happens
obscure authors don’t resolve cleanly

distributional steering (from alignment research) Some work explicitly studies how prompts steer behavior without reasoning.
Often called:

steering vectors
control tokens
soft prompts
prefix tuning

This literature mostly focuses on engineering control, not why cultural labels work so well.
discourse and compression (from sociology and philosophy)
Touches on ideas from:

discourse analysis
sociology of knowledge
semiotics

In these fields, names like “Hemingway” are understood as:

Socially stabilized shorthand for a complex, contested, but repeatable practice.

LLMs inherit that shorthand because they ingest discourse. This connection is rarely made explicit in ML papers.
The missing unifying term
There is no canonical term yet, but here are the closest contenders—and why none fully fit:

Style transfer – too narrow and surface-level.
Prompt engineering – ignores cultural origin.
Latent concept activation – accurate but abstract.
Distributional steering – good, but generic.
Semantic indexing – close, but underspecified socially.

Proposed terms:

Discursive indexing of latent distributions

or

Cultural compression as model control



References and suggested readings (organized by what they help with)

Mechanism primers (conditioning, representation) — EXTRACTED: sections 1-2 (Mikolov, Ethayarajh, Kim, Elazar, Hewitt)
Prompting and control (prompt programming, soft prompts, steering) — EXTRACTED: section 4 (Brown, Reynolds & McDonell, Lester)
Interpretability analogies (feature mixtures/superposition as intuition support) — EXTRACTED: section 3 (Olah, Carter, Geiger)
Style control literature (style transfer, controllable generation) — EXTRACTED: section 6 (Su, Ficler & Goldberg, Ippolito)
Media/discourse theory bridge (where you’ll add journalism-school-native theory readings if you choose) NOTE: Full reference list extracted. Needs reorganization per new category scheme.

3. Interpretability, feature superposition, and emergent structure

Others

Researchers closest to this framing (for context):

Chris Olah (feature superposition, abstraction)

Why this is under-discussed:

It cuts across disciplines (ML + literary theory + sociology).
It challenges the “reasoning” narrative people want LLMs to have.
It suggests models are mirrors of cultural compression, not agents.

title: Specifying a custom Jekyll site location in GitHub pages
text:
If you move the root of your Jekyll project to a subdirectory of a Github Pages enabled repository, you will need to be sure to change the source and destination locations for the jekyll-build-pages action accordingly.
But, that’s not all. It is not obvious, but you also need to specify the path to the upload-pages-artifact action. Failure to do this will result in a GitHub Actions build fail for the “Uplodad artifact” stage, with the error message: “Error: Process completed with exit code 2” (see screenshot below).



Build fail screenshot


The reason this configuration requirement is not obvious is because the path parameter is not defined in the auto-generated Action config that GitHub gives you for Jekyll pages, but instead utilizes the default value of ./_site. To specify the path, use the with > path syntax.
For example, I have moved my Jekyll root to a location called site. Thus, the jobs section of my .github/workflows/pages.yml looks like this, (with special attention paid to the jekyll-build-pages, and the upload-pages-artifact actions):
jobs:
  # Build job
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - name: Setup Pages
        uses: actions/configure-pages@v2
      - name: Build with Jekyll
        uses: actions/jekyll-build-pages@v1
        with:
          source: ./site/
          destination: ./site/_site
      - name: Upload artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v1
        with:
          path: ./site/_site

title: Using urllib to wget a file in Python 3
text:
I used to use python3-wget to fetch remote files if I needed to save them to my filesystem from Python code.
Anymore, I am finding that urllib and shutil used together will do the trick:

import os
import shutil
import urllib
import urllib.request


def download(url, tofile=None):
    """Fetch a file from `url` and save it to `tofile`.
    """
    if tofile is None:
        tofile = os.path.basename(urllib.parse.urlparse(url).path)
    with urllib.request.urlopen(url) as response, open(tofile, "wb") as outfile:
        shutil.copyfileobj(response, outfile)
    return tofile

title: Fixing Git LFS Integration in Obsidian’s Git Plugin on macOS
text:
If you’re using the Obsidian Git plugin with Git LFS (Large File Storage) on macOS, you may run into a frustrating issue: git-lfs works fine in your terminal, but Obsidian Git throws this error:
Even after running git lfs install and configuring .gitattributes correctly, Git LFS fails — and so do pushes involving large files.

title: Fixing Git LFS Integration in Obsidian’s Git Plugin on macOS
section: 🧠 The Problem: GUI apps ignore your shell config
text:
🧠 The Problem: GUI apps ignore your shell config
When you launch Obsidian from the Dock, Spotlight, or Finder:

It does not load your ~/.zprofile, ~/.bashrc, or config.fish.
It does not inherit the same $PATH you see in your terminal.
So anything installed in /usr/local/bin, like git-lfs, is invisible to Obsidian’s Git plugin.

title: Fixing Git LFS Integration in Obsidian’s Git Plugin on macOS
section: ✅ The Solution: A Custom .app Launcher With a Fixed $PATH
text:
✅ The Solution: A Custom .app Launcher With a Fixed $PATH
Instead of trying to patch macOS’s environment or launchctl quirks, the simplest and most reliable fix is:

🛠 Build a wrapper .app that launches Obsidian with the correct environment.

This gives you:

A real clickable macOS app
The ability to fix $PATH permanently
A proper app icon and dock integration

title: Native Desktop Notifications for tmux Events
text:
When you’re deep in a tmux session with multiple windows, you lose visibility into background activity. A build finishes in one window, a process crashes in another, and you don’t notice until you manually cycle through tabs. Desktop notifications solve this — but getting them to work well, with click-to-navigate-to-source, turned out to be a multi-day exploration across five different approaches.
The result is tmux-notify, a standalone Rust binary that sends native desktop notifications for tmux events and opens a new terminal window at the source when you click them.
This post walks through the exploration: what didn’t work, why, and what ultimately did.

title: Native Desktop Notifications for tmux Events
text:
The Goal
Wire tmux hooks (bell, pane-died, etc.) to desktop notifications. When the user clicks a notification, navigate them to the tmux session and window where the event occurred.
The ideal flow:

Event fires in a background tmux window
System notification appears (visible regardless of which app is focused)
User clicks the notification
A terminal opens, attached to the source session, on the correct window

title: Native Desktop Notifications for tmux Events
text:
Attempt 1: OSC Terminal Escape Sequences
Terminal emulators support OSC (Operating System Command) escape sequences for notifications — OSC 777, OSC 9, OSC 99, depending on the emulator. The appeal is obvious: no external dependencies, just echo a magic string to the terminal.
# OSC 777 notification
printf '\e]777;notify;Build Failed;See window cargo\e\\'
This doesn’t work for two reasons. First, Ghostty (and other emulators) suppresses notification banners when the terminal is focused. The assumption is that if you’re looking at the terminal, you don’t need a notification about it. But tmux multiplexes — a background window event while you’re focused on a foreground one is exactly when you need a notification.
Second, OSC notifications are fire-and-forget. There’s no click callback mechanism. You can’t navigate to the source.

title: Native Desktop Notifications for tmux Events
section: Attempt 2: System Notifications via External Tools
text:
Attempt 2: System Notifications via External Tools
The next approach used system-level notification tools: alerter and terminal-notifier on macOS, notify-send on Linux. These produce real OS notifications that appear regardless of which application has focus.
alerter was the strongest option on macOS:
alerter --title "Build Failed" \
        --message "exit code 1" \
        --timeout 30 \
        --actions "Go to source" \
        --json
It blocks until click/dismiss/timeout and returns JSON indicating what the user did. On click, we run tmux select-window and then open a terminal.
This worked well. The drawback was requiring users to install external tools.

title: Native Desktop Notifications for tmux Events
text:
The “Find Existing Window” Dead End
Before settling on the click handler design, we spent time trying to find and activate the user’s existing terminal window containing the tmux session. The idea seems intuitive — why open a new window when there’s already one right there?
It’s fundamentally broken:

No universal API. Each terminal emulator has different (or no) window management IPC. Ghostty has no programmatic window control at all.
Tab identification is impossible. Even if you can enumerate windows, there’s no reliable way to determine which tab is attached to which tmux session.
Focus stealing. Activating the wrong window is worse than doing nothing.
Race conditions. The window may have closed between identification and activation.

The breakthrough: always open a new terminal window. tmux attach -t <session> is instant for an existing session. The user gets a fresh window showing exactly the right content. Close it with Cmd+W when done. Simple, reliable, predictable.

title: Native Desktop Notifications for tmux Events
section: Attempt 3: Native macOS Notifications with objc2

title: Native Desktop Notifications for tmux Events
text:
Terminal Dispatch
The click handler needs to open a terminal attached to the right tmux session. Each emulator has different invocation patterns, and macOS requires open -na for GUI apps.








Terminal
macOS
Linux




Ghostty
open -na Ghostty.app --args --command=<cmd>
ghostty -e bash -c <cmd>


Alacritty
open -na Alacritty.app --args -e <tmux> attach
alacritty -e <tmux> attach


Kitty
open -na kitty.app --args bash -c <cmd>
kitty -e bash -c <cmd>


WezTerm
open -na WezTerm.app --args start -- bash -c
wezterm start -- bash -c


iTerm2
osascript create window with command
—


Terminal.app
osascript do script
—


foot
—
foot -e bash -c <cmd>



Ghostty’s --quit-after-last-window-closed=true flag ensures the spawned instance exits cleanly when the user closes the window.

title: Native Desktop Notifications for tmux Events
text:
Linux: notify-send
Linux support is straightforward. notify-send from libnotify 0.8+ supports --action and --wait:
notify-send "Build Failed" "exit code 1" \
    --action=source="Go to source" \
    --wait
The process blocks until interaction. If the user clicks “Go to source”, stdout contains source, and we proceed with the same select-window + open-terminal flow.

title: Native Desktop Notifications for tmux Events
section: Wiring It Up with tmux Hooks
text:
Wiring It Up with tmux Hooks
The final piece is tmux hook installation. Two hooks cover the most useful events:
# Bell: fires when a program sends BEL (e.g., build tools on completion)
tmux set-hook -t $SESSION alert-bell \
    "run-shell -b 'tmux-notify \
        \"Bell: #{session_name} ▸ #{window_name}\" \"\" \
        --session #{session_name} --window #{window_name}'"

# Pane exit: fires when a pane's process terminates
tmux set-option -w -t $SESSION remain-on-exit on
tmux set-hook -w -t $SESSION pane-died \
    "run-shell -b 'tmux-notify \
        \"Exited: #{session_name} ▸ #{window_name}\" \
        \"exit #{pane_dead_status}\" \
        --session #{session_name} --window #{window_name}'"
The run-shell -b flag is critical — without it, the blocking notification process freezes the tmux hook.

title: Native Desktop Notifications for tmux Events
text:
Repository
The full implementation is at github.com/scott2b/notifications-demo. It includes setup scripts for the macOS .app bundle, a tmux hook installer, an end-to-end demo, and detailed architecture and retrospective documentation.
